title: Addressing envelopes in italic
subtitle: Applying italic calligraphy to address blocks and envelope layout
excerpt: Addressing envelopes in italic is one of the most practical uses of calligraphy. The scale is small, the format is constrained, and the writing must be legible at a reading distance of 30 to 40 cm. The italic chancery hand is well suited: it is both formal and readable.
sourceNotes: Italic calligraphy for envelope addressing is a practical application of the chancery italic hand described in Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), and in subsequent italic revival literature. Technique synthesised from standard italic practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A 15th-century Italian court hand. Characterised by a slight 5 to 10 degree forward lean, compressed letterforms, and occasional exit flourishes. The basis of what is now called italic handwriting.

content.text: adds a practical constraint to a learned skill. The text must fit the address block, read clearly at normal reading distance, and meet postal guidelines for machine-readable addresses. These constraints help rather than hinder: they give the work a clear structure.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 6 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-6)
items.description: The finest in the Roundhand series. For envelope addressing, small and legible is the requirement. A nib-6 italic at 4 nib-widths x-height gives an x-height of approximately 4 mm, which reads clearly on a standard C5 envelope.

items.name: Iron-gall ink (slug: iron-gall-ink)
items.description: The most legible ink colour; black on white reads under all postal lighting conditions.

items.name: Tracing paper (slug: tracing-paper)
items.description: For planning the layout before writing on the envelope.

items.name: Light box (slug: light-box-a4)
items.description: Optional. Place a ruled guide-line sheet under the envelope and use the light box to see the lines through the envelope.

content.text: Count the lines of the address. A standard UK address runs four to six lines. Allow 7 to 8 mm per line at nib-6 italic. Six lines gives a total height of about 45 mm, which fits in the lower centre of a C5 envelope.

Sketch the layout on tracing paper first: mark the line positions and test the name line width against the longest line of the address. Left-aligned addresses are easier to execute consistently than centred addresses; centred looks more formal but requires calculating the width of each line in advance.

Rule light pencil guide lines directly on the envelope if you are not using a light box guide-line sheet. The lines will erase cleanly once the ink is fully dry. Write the name line first; it carries the most visual weight. Keep the nib angle at 45 degrees and the letter lean at 5 to 10 degrees from vertical.

Numbers in an italic address are written at the same angle and nib angle as the letters. Postcode letters and numbers mix freely; keep consistent x-height and spacing. Leave slightly more space between the postcode line and the town line than between the other lines, the postcode needs to be machine-readable and visual separation helps.

Wait at least 15 minutes after the last ink stroke before erasing guide lines. Iron-gall ink continues to dry and bond to the paper surface for several minutes. Erasing too soon lifts the ink surface. Use a soft eraser with light strokes along the line rather than across it.
